categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's positioning for the next 6–12 months across fundamental, macro, cycle, technical, catalyst, and flow lenses. This category is explicitly forward-looking but NOT predictive — it assesses whether the fund is favorably positioned for the current and expected regime and identifies the dominant tailwinds, headwinds, and catalysts retail investors should watch. Pass/Fail on each factor means 'favorably positioned' vs 'poorly positioned' given the regime and expected path, not a price target or return forecast. Because the category requires synthesizing current market conditions, the prompt expects and encourages web lookup of macro indicators, valuation multiples, rate-path expectations, credit spreads, sector flows, volatility regime, and upcoming policy/earnings catalysts — provided data blocks alone are insufficient here. Factors are assigned to one or more ETF groups defined in etf-analysis-categories.json; each group uses exactly 5 factors from this file.

factors.factorDescription: Evaluates whether the fund's underlying holdings are currently cheap, fairly valued, or expensive relative to their own history and to peer categories. For equity funds the lens is forward P/E, P/B, P/S, P/E-to-growth, and dividend yield versus the index or sub-category's own 5–10 year historical range — a sector trading in the top decile of its own valuation range has thin room for multiple expansion; a sector in the bottom quartile has more upside room if fundamentals support it. For fixed-income funds the lens is SEC yield versus its own 5–10 year range, real yield (SEC yield minus market breakeven inflation) versus historical norms, and whether carry adequately compensates for current duration risk. For muni funds add tax-equivalent yield at a stated bracket (e.g. ~32% federal) versus comparable taxable bonds. Valuation is a framing tool, not a market-timing tool — expensive markets can stay expensive for long periods — but it tells you how much margin of error you have. Pass when the underlying valuation sits at or below category median AND there is fundamental support for current prices, OR when valuations are rich but fundamental trajectory is clearly strong enough to justify them. Fail when valuations sit in the top decile of their own history without corresponding earnings or cash-flow strength, implying the next 12 months will likely need multiple compression. For young funds or newly-constructed thematic indices without a long valuation history, compare to the closest broad index or sub-category peer.
factors.factorMetrics: indexName, overviewCategory, peRatio, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, duration, topHoldings, strategyText

factors.factorDescription: Evaluates the earnings, revenue, margin, and cash-flow trajectory of the fund's underlying holdings for equity exposures, or the credit-quality trajectory for bond exposures. For broad-equity and sector/thematic funds the read is whether the top holdings are collectively showing rising versus falling earnings revisions, expanding versus compressing margins, and positive versus negative free-cash-flow momentum over the past 1–3 quarters. A strong trajectory (rising revisions, expanding margins, positive operating leverage) supports multiple expansion or at minimum defends current valuation; a weak trajectory (downward revisions, margin pressure, gross-margin compression) is a headwind regardless of starting valuation. For credit holdings inside bond funds the equivalent read is the ratio of rating upgrades to downgrades in the underlying credit-quality tiers, and the trend in default rates versus 12 months ago. For passive index funds the trajectory is mechanically the weighted composite of the index's holdings — judge the composite, not individual names. For allocation and target-date funds judge both the equity sleeve and the credit sleeve separately and note the direction of the weighted average. Pass when the weighted fundamental trajectory is flat or improving across a majority of the top holdings. Fail when there is a clear material deterioration (e.g. top-10 holdings collectively seeing earnings-estimate cuts of 10%+ over the past quarter, or rising downgrade ratio in the credit sleeve).

Assesses whether the fund's exposure profile is well-positioned for the current and expected macro regime over the next 6–12 months. The macro frame has four axes retail investors can grasp: growth (expanding, slowing, contracting), inflation (rising, sticky, falling), monetary policy (tightening, holding, easing), and financial conditions (easy, tight). Each fund type has a preferred regime: growth and large-cap tech do well in easy-money + falling-inflation + expanding-growth setups; value and dividend equity do better in rising-rate + sticky-inflation regimes; long-duration Treasuries rally in falling-rate + slowing-growth regimes; credit-risk assets (high yield, bank loan, preferred, EM debt) need stable or expanding growth AND narrowing spreads; gold and broad commodities typically benefit from negative real rates and a weakening dollar; managed-futures benefit from sustained trends in either direction; market-neutral and multi-strategy alt funds need dispersion and moderate volatility. Name the current regime explicitly using available macro indicators (CPI, PMIs, unemployment, Fed stance, yield-curve shape) and frame whether it matches the fund's preferred setup. Pass when the current and expected regime aligns with the fund's structural tilt. Fail when the regime is clearly working against the fund (e.g., long-duration Treasury fund in a re-accelerating inflation + hawkish-Fed setup, or low-vol equity fund in a rapidly expanding risk-on setup).
factors.factorMetrics: overviewCategory, assetClass, indexName, duration, beta, strategyText

factors.factorDescription: For rate-sensitive funds, measures how the market-implied rate path — Fed dot-plot versus SOFR / OIS-implied cuts and hikes, yield-curve shape, breakeven inflation — interacts with the fund's duration. A long-duration fund (duration >10) rallies hard if rates fall ~100 bps and loses 10%+ if rates rise ~100 bps; a short-duration fund (duration <3) is relatively insulated from rate surprises but earns less carry; an intermediate fund (duration 5–7) is the compromise. The yield-curve shape also matters: an inverted curve (2y above 10y) implies the market already expects cuts — funds positioned further out the curve benefit more when cuts actually arrive, while a flat curve argues for short duration + credit tilt. For floating-rate funds (bank loan, floating-rate preferreds) duration is effectively near-zero so the fund benefits from sustained high short rates and loses less than fixed-coupon peers when rates rise. For allocation and target-date funds the bond-sleeve duration drives the overall rate sensitivity. Pass when the fund's duration matches or benefits from the market-implied 12-month rate path. Fail when the fund's duration is clearly offside versus the implied path (e.g., long duration while the front-end is repricing hawkish, or short duration while the market is pricing aggressive cuts).
factors.factorMetrics: duration, overviewCategory, indexName, beta, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, overviewSecYield

factors.factorDescription: For credit-sensitive bond funds, evaluates where we are in the credit cycle and how current spread levels position the fund. Credit spreads (the extra yield corporate / high-yield / EM / muni bonds pay above Treasuries) tighten when the economy is expanding and defaults are falling — this produces capital gains on top of carry; spreads widen during slowdowns and recessions — causing capital losses and sometimes default losses on top. The framework: early-cycle (widening spreads + Fed cutting) is historically the strongest buy setup for high-yield and credit; mid-cycle (stable spreads + Fed on hold) is a collect-the-carry regime; late-cycle (tight spreads + Fed tightening + rising defaults) argues for carry-only exposure and defensive tiering; recession (wide spreads + Fed cutting) is the best long-term entry but painful getting there. Compare current HY OAS (option-adjusted spread), IG spreads, and muni credit spreads versus their own 10-year medians — spreads in the tightest quintile of history are a yellow flag because compensation for credit risk is thin. Pass when spreads are at or above long-run median AND the default outlook is stable or improving. Fail when spreads are in the tightest quintile AND the credit cycle is late-stage with rising default forecasts and tightening bank-lending standards.

For sector and thematic ETFs, evaluates where the specific sector or theme sits in its own cycle. Each sector has its own rhythm: technology leads early and can peak mid-to-late cycle; financials benefit from rising long rates and healthy credit; energy rides commodity cycles and policy shifts; utilities and consumer staples outperform late-cycle and in recessions; real estate tracks the rate cycle and vacancy dynamics; healthcare outperforms in defensive regimes but carries policy-risk overlays; semiconductors have their own inventory/capacity cycle distinct from broad tech. Thematic funds (clean energy, cybersecurity, AI, robotics, infrastructure, digital-asset equities) need both secular-theme durability AND a read on the narrative's current hype vs hangover phase. The four-phase framework: accumulation (underowned, improving fundamentals, rising relative strength), markup (trending, consensus building, valuations expanding), distribution (consensus positive but breadth narrowing, flow momentum flagging), markdown (downtrending, outflows, capitulation setting up). Pass when the sector or theme is in accumulation or early-markup with fundamental support and improving flows. Fail when it is in distribution with narrowing breadth, or in active markdown with deteriorating flows and fundamentals. Thematic hype peaks (sudden AUM surge + narrative saturation + stretched valuations) are a specific red flag to name when present.
factors.factorMetrics: overviewCategory, indexName, sectorBreakdown, topHoldings, ma50, ma200, rsiM, strategyText

factors.factorDescription: For funds where a 3–12 month trade-timing read matters (leveraged / inverse and sector / thematic tactical tilts), evaluates the current technical setup. Inputs: price vs MA20 / MA50 / MA150 / MA200; MA50 vs MA200 (golden-cross or death-cross); RSI daily (short-term overbought/oversold), RSI weekly (intermediate momentum), RSI monthly (long-term regime); distance from 52-week high and low, ATH and ATL. Patterns: price above all four MAs + weekly RSI 50–70 + monthly RSI rising = healthy uptrend; price above MAs but weekly RSI >70 with bearish daily divergence = stretched / pullback risk; price below MA50 with MA50 < MA200 + weekly RSI < 40 = confirmed downtrend; price below all MAs with monthly RSI < 30 = oversold, wait for basing action before long entry. This factor is most decision-useful for leveraged and inverse funds (where timing is the product) and for sector / thematic funds where entry point matters materially. For broad-equity passive, long-horizon bond, muni, and allocation funds, technicals are a secondary input — do not over-weight them. Pass when the setup supports the fund's directional mandate (uptrend for long, confirmed downtrend for inverse). Fail when the setup is clearly offside (e.g., daily-leveraged long in a confirmed downtrend, or inverse fund in a strong uptrend), or when momentum is stretched enough to imply near-term pullback risk greater than the remaining upside.
factors.factorMetrics: stockPrice, ma20, ma50, ma150, ma200, ma50ChgPercent, ma200ChgPercent, rsi, rsiW, rsiM, high52wChg, low52wChg, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent

factors.factorDescription: Identifies specific events in the next 30–90 days that could meaningfully reprice the fund, either up or down, and frames them as tailwinds or headwinds given the fund's current positioning. Catalyst categories: (1) monetary policy — Fed / ECB / BoJ meetings, scheduled CPI and employment prints, PCE, PMI surveys; (2) fiscal and political — elections, tax policy changes (critical for muni), regulatory and antitrust decisions affecting specific sectors; (3) earnings season — timing of top-holding earnings, especially for concentrated thematic funds; (4) commodity supply and demand — OPEC+ decisions, weather-driven demand shifts, geopolitical supply shocks; (5) fund-specific mechanics — defined-outcome buffer reset dates, options expiry cycles for covered-call / derivative income funds, index rebalancing; (6) technical / flow events — quarter-end and year-end rebalancing, fund-flow seasonality, major option expiry cycles. The deliverable is to name the 2–4 most relevant catalysts, say whether each is likely a tailwind or headwind given the fund's positioning, and state approximate dates. Pass when the identified catalysts are net favorable or mixed-but-manageable. Fail when the dominant near-term catalyst is clearly working against the fund (e.g., a long-duration Treasury fund heading into a hot-CPI window where a hawkish Fed response would hit it, or a sector ETF whose top-5 holdings all report earnings in the next 30 days with mostly negative pre-announcements).

For income-heavy funds, evaluates whether the distribution stream is sustainable at current levels over the next 12 months — not just whether the headline yield is high. For core credit, high-yield, bank loan, preferred, and EM debt funds, check whether current yield is covered by earned income (SEC yield close to distribution yield is a healthy sign; distribution yield materially above SEC yield usually means the fund is paying above what it earns, which is unsustainable); for credit funds frame the yield premium against expected defaults; for muni apply tax-equivalent yield at a stated bracket (e.g. ~32% federal) and note in-state tax benefits where relevant. For covered-call and derivative-income funds the distribution is mostly options premium harvested by selling calls — sustainability depends on the volatility regime, because a compressing VIX reduces the premium available and typically forces either yield compression or drift into return-of-capital (ROC). Distribution composition is critical: high ROC percentage is not 'free yield' — it reduces cost basis and is really a return of principal. Also watch distribution trend: has the fund cut its distribution in the past 12 months? Pass when the yield is mostly earned, the distribution composition is primarily ordinary income / qualified dividends / option premium / carry, and the yield-generation engine remains viable in the current regime. Fail when the headline yield is propped up by ROC, the strategy's yield engine is compressing (e.g., covered-call fund in a sustained low-VIX regime), or the distribution has been cut meaningfully over the past 12 months.
factors.factorMetrics: dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, divYears, divGrYears, overviewCategory, strategyText

factors.factorDescription: Evaluates fund-flow dynamics and broader positioning in the fund's category / theme as either a confirmation or contrarian signal. Three lenses: (1) fund-level flows — is AUM rising or falling over the past 3–12 months, and is the flow trend matching or diverging from performance; a fund with strong performance and steady outflows is a warning sign, a fund with weak performance and steady inflows suggests buy-the-dip conviction; (2) category-level positioning — is the Mor category seeing net inflows (consensus long, crowded risk when valuations are also stretched) or net outflows (out-of-favor, potentially setting up for eventual reversal); (3) speculator positioning in the underlying — CFTC Commitments of Traders for commodity and currency funds, short interest as % of float for leveraged/inverse, put/call ratios and AAII sentiment for broad indices. Heavy inflows into an already-expensive category is a textbook crowded-long setup where future 12-month returns tend to disappoint. Heavy outflows from a cheap category is a late-stage capitulation setup that often precedes reversal. Pass when flows are confirming the fund's fundamental setup (inflows + fair valuation + improving fundamentals) OR when flows are creating a contrarian opportunity (persistent outflows + cheap valuation + stabilizing fundamentals). Fail when flows are confirming a stretched crowded-long trade at expensive levels, OR when sustained outflows combined with thin volume are dragging the fund toward closure risk.
factors.factorMetrics: aum, volume, avgVolume, dollarVol, overviewCategory, assetClass

factors.factorDescription: For volatility-sensitive funds (leveraged / inverse, options-based derivative income, managed futures, volatility-targeting alt), evaluates the current and expected volatility regime over the next 3–6 months. Regimes and their consequences: calm (VIX < 15, low realized volatility, smooth trending markets) helps long-equity leverage, hurts covered-call yield because there is less premium to harvest, and is neutral-to-negative for managed futures if trends are weak; normal (VIX 15–20, moderate realized volatility, typical two-way tape) is broadly balanced across strategies; elevated (VIX 20–30, choppy two-way tape with whipsaws) devastates daily-reset leveraged funds through compounding decay, boosts covered-call income because option premia are rich, and helps managed futures only if trends persist through the chop; crisis (VIX > 30, persistent directional stress, very high realized volatility) is hostile to leveraged-long and short-vol strategies and can help inverse products in a sustained directional decline. Also watch the implied vs realized volatility spread — when implied is materially above realized, premium-selling strategies (covered-call, defined-outcome) are attractive; when realized is above implied, long-vol strategies benefit. Pass when the current and expected vol regime is favorable for the fund's structural volatility exposure. Fail when the regime is hostile (e.g., daily-leveraged fund heading into a choppy elevated-vol regime where daily-reset decay compounds painfully, or covered-call fund in a sustained low-VIX regime where income has compressed).
factors.factorMetrics: beta, beta1y, atr, drawdown, overviewCategory, strategyText
